input.prompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.content:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

<current_datetime>2026-05-02T00:57:41.671Z</current_datetime>

We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

input.initialPrompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.reasoningText:
The user migrated from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform (MTP) and is hitting exit code 8 when test assemblies produce zero tests. This is a known behavioral difference — MTP returns a non-zero exit code when no tests are discovered, whereas VSTest treated that as success.

Let me look at their project files to understand their setup, and also invoke the relevant skill for MTP migration guidance.

data.content:
This is a well-known MTP behavioral difference. **Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns exit code 8 when zero tests are discovered**, whereas VSTest returned 0. Since your tests are behind `#if INCLUDE_SLOW_TESTS`, configurations without that symbol produce zero tests → exit code 8.

**Fix:** Tell MTP to treat "no tests found" as success by adding this property to your `.csproj`:

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- MTP returns exit code 8 when no tests are discovered; treat that as success
         so conditional-compilation configurations that produce zero tests don't fail CI. -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>

<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- MTP returns exit code 8 when no tests are discovered; treat that as success
         so conditional-compilation configurations that produce zero tests don't fail CI. -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>

data.content:
The fix works. Even though MTP reports "Failed! … Total: 0", **the process now exits with code 0** instead of 8.

### What happened

| Behavior | VSTest | MTP (default) | MTP (with fix) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Zero tests discovered | exit 0 ✅ | exit 8 ❌ | exit 0 ✅ |

### What was added

```xml
<TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>
```

This MSBuild property tells MTP to return exit code **0** when no tests are found, matching the old VSTest behavior. Your conditional-compilation configurations will no longer break CI.

**Alternative (CI-side):** If you prefer not to change the project file, you can set the environment variable `TESTINGPLATFORM_EXITCODE_IGNORE=8` in your pipeline instead.
